beats.text: You've been using the commutative property to solve problems, with arrays, without arrays, in all kinds of situations. Let's step back and connect what you discovered.

condition.selected: Both methods give the same expression

incorrects.description: Student selected B: focused on the equation result rather than the structural anchor.

params.description: Left array highlights as a complete set.
Right array highlights as a complete set.

steps.text: The expression is the result, not the reason. Look at the arrays themselves. What stayed exactly the same in both demonstrations?

incorrects.description: Student selected C: incorrect focus on method detail rather than structural invariant.

params.description: Left array: rows flash briefly to show row method was also used.

steps.text: Actually, the left side showed rows first, then columns. The method isn't what matters. Focus on the dots themselves. What didn't change?

incorrects.description: Student answered incorrectly. System models the correct answer.

params.description: Left array: all dots pulse to emphasize total count remains 20.
Right array: all dots pulse to emphasize total count remains 20.

steps.text: Here's what matters. On the left, we looked at the same dots a different way. On the right, we turned the same dots. But in both cases, the dots themselves never changed. No dots added, no dots removed. Same dots, same total. That's the anchor. The answer is: Same dots, nothing is added or removed.

incorrects.description: Student selected 79, treating the problem as concatenation rather than multiplication

steps.text: You're thinking about this a bit differently. Here's a hint: 9 times 7 equals 63. The turn-around fact means 7 times 9 equals the same product.

incorrects.description: Student selected 56, likely computing 7×8 or misremembering the fact

steps.text: Let's think about this together. We know 9 times 7 is 63. When we switch the factors around, we get the same product. 7 times 9 also equals 63.

condition.selected: I'd need to see the array

incorrects.description: Student selected need to see array, not yet trusting the property without visual support

steps.text: Here's what helps: you've seen that rotating an array doesn't change the total dots. That means the turn-around facts work even when you can't see the array. If 9 times 7 equals 63, then 7 times 9 equals 63 too.

incorrects.description: Student answered incorrectly. System models the correct answer.

steps.text: Let me show you how this works. We know 9 times 7 equals 63. When we switch the factors, we get 7 times 9. The turn-around facts tell us this equals the same product: 63. You don't need an array because the property always works. The answer is 63.

incorrects.description: Student selected only for small numbers, limiting the property to a subset of numbers

steps.text: You're working on it. The key is that you could imagine an array for ANY two numbers. Whether it's 3 times 4 or 99 times 47, rotating the array always gives you the same dots. The property works for all numbers, not just small ones.

condition.selected: Only when you have an array

incorrects.description: Student selected only when you have an array, not yet seeing property as independent of visualization

steps.text: Here's a hint: you can always imagine an array, even when you don't draw one. Whether you see 5 times 8 as rows and columns or not, switching the factors still gives you the same product. The property works even when you don't have an array in front of you.

condition.selected: Only when I know the first multiplication fact

incorrects.description: Student selected only when I know the first fact, missing that property is structural not memorization-dependent

steps.text: Let's think about this a bit more. The turn-around facts aren't about what you've memorized. They're about how multiplication itself works. Any two numbers, whether you know the fact or not, will give you the same product when you switch them. That's what makes it a property.

incorrects.description: Student answered incorrectly. System models the correct answer.

steps.text: Let me walk you through this one. Imagine any two numbers, like 9 and 7. You could build an array with 9 rows and 7 columns. Now rotate it: 7 rows and 9 columns. Same dots, same product. This works for ANY two numbers, big or small, whether you see an array or just imagine one. That's why it's called a property. It's always true. The answer is yes, it always works.

condition.selected: If I know 5 × 7, I should still compute 7 × 5 separately to be sure

incorrects.description: Student selected option B: computing both facts separately

params.description: The fact pair 3 × 8 = 24 and 8 × 3 = 24 pulses gently.

steps.text: You're working on it. The key is that you don't need to compute both. Look at 3 × 8 and 8 × 3. They give the same product. One fact tells you both answers.

condition.selected: The strategy only helps when I have an array in front of me

incorrects.description: Student selected option C: limiting strategy to visual contexts

params.description: Text '3 facts learned → 6 facts known' pulses.

steps.text: Let's think about this together. The commutative property works with any multiplication, not just arrays. Whether you see a picture or just numbers, 5 × 7 and 7 × 5 both equal 35.

incorrects.description: Student answered incorrectly. System models the correct answer.

params.description: Option A highlights: 'If I know 5 × 7, I already know 7 × 5 — no extra work'.

steps.text: Here, let me show you. The strategy is about saving work. When you know 5 × 7 equals 35, you automatically know 7 × 5 equals 35 too. No extra computing needed. The answer is the first option. That is what makes it a strategy, it lets you skip work you would otherwise have to do.

params.description: Brief montage or summary display showing the progression: equal groups (bags/circles), expressions, equations, arrays, commutative property.

beats.text: Think about where you started. You found equal groups in bags and circles. You learned that multiplication means groups of the same size. You built expressions, then equations. You discovered arrays: organized groups with rows and columns. And today, you proved something powerful: you can multiply in any order and always get the same answer.

elements.description: Summary display showing progression: equal groups, expressions, equations, arrays, commutative property.

beats.text: You didn't just memorize that. You proved it with highlighting and rotation. You used it as a strategy. That's real mathematical thinking.

elements.description: Summary display showing progression: equal groups, expressions, equations, arrays, commutative property.
Summary display showing progression: equal groups, expressions, equations, arrays, commutative property.
